title: compilor design
content:
Introduction to left recursion


  • Is session me Left Recursion ko eliminate karne ke solved examples dekhenge.

  • Chaliye start karte hain learning.

Session Outcome

  • Aaj hum 4 solved examples dekhenge jisme Left Recursion eliminate kiya jayega.

Example 1

Given Production

P → P + Q | Q

Identify Components

  • P = generic non-terminal A

  • Right side me P leftmost position par dubara aa raha hai

  • Isliye Left Recursive Production hai.

Alpha aur Beta

  • α (alpha) = + Q

  • β (beta) = Q

Left Recursion Removal Rule

A → β A′
A′ → α A′ | ε

Apply Rule

  • P → Q P′

  • P′ → + Q P′ | ε

Result

  • Ab P′ rightmost position par aa raha hai

  • Isliye grammar Right Recursive ban gaya.

Example 2

Given Grammar

S → S 0 S 1 S | 01

Identify Parts

  • S = A

  • Right side me S leftmost hai → Left Recursion.

Alpha aur Beta

  • α = 0 S 1 S

  • β = 01

Convert Grammar

Start Symbol:
S → 01 S′

New Non-terminal:
S′ → 0 S 1 S S′ | ε

Result

  • S′ rightmost position par hai

  • Grammar Right Recursive ho gaya.

Example 3

Given Grammar

A → ( B ) | B
B → B * A | A

Observation

  • First production (A) me left recursion nahi hai.

  • Second production (B) me left recursion hai.

Identify Components

  • B = A type non-terminal

  • α = * A

  • β = A

Convert Grammar

B → A B′

B′ → * A B′ | ε

Final Modified Grammar

A → ( B ) | B
B → A B′
B′ → * A B′ | ε

Example 4 (General Case)

Given Grammar

A → Aα1 | Aα2 | Aα3 | … | β1 | β2 | β3 | …

Identify Left Recursion

  • Aα1, Aα2, Aα3 wali productions left recursive hain

  • Kyunki A leftmost position par aa raha hai.

Convert Grammar

A → β1 A′ | β2 A′ | β3 A′ | …

A′ → α1 A′ | α2 A′ | α3 A′ | … | ε

Result

  • A′ rightmost position par hai

  • Grammar Right Recursive ban gaya.

Important Concept

  • Left Recursion eliminate karna zaroori hai

  • Kyunki Top-Down Parsers left recursive grammars ko handle nahi kar pate.

  • Wo confuse ho jaate hain aur grammar reject kar dete hain.

Session Summary

Is session me humne:

  • Left Recursion elimination

  • 4 solved examples

  • General formula

seekha.


Non-Deterministic Context Free Grammar – Notes (Hinglish)

1. Session ka Goal

Aaj ke session me 3 main cheeze seekhenge:

  1. Context Free Grammars (CFG) ki classifications

  2. Non-determinism kya hota hai

  3. Left Factoring procedure

2. CFG ki Different Classifications

Ab tak humne CFG ke kuch types dekhe hain:

1️⃣ Ambiguous CFG

  • Ek string ke multiple parse trees ho sakte hain.

2️⃣ Unambiguous CFG

  • Har string ka sirf ek hi parse tree hota hai.

📌 Parsers ko unambiguous grammar chahiye hoti hai.
Ambiguity se associativity aur precedence violate ho sakte hain.

3️⃣ Left Recursive CFG

Production ka form:

A → Aα

Yaha left side par same non-terminal se start hota hai.

📌 Top-down parsers me problem create karta hai.

4️⃣ Right Recursive CFG

Production ka form:

A → αA

📌 Top-down parsers right recursive grammars ko easily accept karte hain.

Ab ek aur classification aata hai:

5️⃣ Non-Deterministic CFG

6️⃣ Deterministic CFG

Aaj hum non-deterministic CFG ko samjhenge.

3. Non-Deterministic CFG

Example form:

A → αβ1
A → αβ2
A → αβ3

Ya likh sakte hain:

A → αβ1 | αβ2 | αβ3

📌 Matlab:

State A se α dekhne par multiple possibilities hain.

4. State Machine Perspective

Agar state machine ki tarah dekhe:

State A se α input par machine ja sakti hai:

  • β1

  • β2

  • β3

Matlab ek input se multiple paths possible hain.

Isi ko non-determinism bolte hain.

5. Top-Down Parser ka Behaviour

Suppose hume derive karna hai:

αβ3

Parser kya karega:

Step 1

Parser first production choose karega

A → αβ1

Derivation:

A → α → β1

But baad me pata chalega yeh correct derivation nahi hai.

Isliye parser backtrack karega.

Step 2

Parser second rule try karega:

A → αβ2

Again derivation:

A → α → β2

Again wrong.

Parser phir backtrack karega.

Step 3

Parser third rule try karega:

A → αβ3

Ab correct string mil jaati hai.

6. Problem: Backtracking

Parser ko:

  • multiple baar try karna padta hai

  • backtracking karni padti hai

📌 Reason:

Sab productions ka same prefix α hai.

Isko bolte hain:

Common Prefix Problem

7. Non-Determinism ka Reason

Machine production ko completely check nahi karti.

Sirf starting symbol (α) dekhkar rule select kar leti hai.

Isliye galat rule choose ho jata hai.

8. Solution: Left Factoring

Non-determinism ko remove karne ke liye Left Factoring use karte hain.

9. Left Factoring Process

Original grammar:

A → αβ1
A → αβ2
A → αβ3

Sabme common prefix α hai.

Isko factor karte hain.

Step 1

Common prefix bahar nikaalo:

A → αA'

Step 2

Remaining parts new non-terminal me daalo:

A' → β1 | β2 | β3

10. New Grammar

Final grammar:

A → αA'
A' → β1 | β2 | β3

11. Result

Ab grammar deterministic ban gayi.

Parser ko:

  • backtracking nahi karni padegi

  • easily correct rule choose karega

12. Important Point (Exam)

📌 Top-down parsers suffer from non-deterministic CFGs

Kyuki:

  • common prefixes

  • backtracking

Isliye Left Factoring use karke non-determinism eliminate karte hain.

13. CFG ki Final Classifications

CFG ko 3 tarah se classify kar sakte hain:

1️⃣ Ambiguity based

  • Ambiguous

  • Unambiguous

2️⃣ Recursion based

  • Left Recursive

  • Right Recursive

3️⃣ Determinism based

  • Non-Deterministic

  • Deterministic

14. Summary

  • Non-deterministic CFG me same prefix wali productions hoti hain.

  • Isse top-down parser me backtracking hoti hai.

  • Problem ka solution hai Left Factoring.

  • Left factoring me common prefix ko factor out karte hain.

Solved Problems on Non-Deterministic CFG – Notes (Hinglish)

1. Session ka Goal

Is session me:

1️⃣ Left Factoring ke solved examples dekhenge
2️⃣ Prove karenge ki Determinism ambiguity ko remove nahi karta

Example 1

Given Grammar

A → aAB
A → aBc
A → aAc

Step 1: Generic Non-Terminal Identify karo

Generic non-terminal = A

Step 2: Common Prefix (α) Find karo

Sab productions observe karo:

A → aAB
A → aBc
A → aAc

Common prefix = a

So

α = a

Step 3: Remaining Parts = β

Common prefix hataane ke baad:

β1 = AB
β2 = Bc
β3 = Ac

Step 4: Left Factoring Apply karo

Original:

A → aAB | aBc | aAc

Common prefix a bahar nikaalenge.

New rule:

A → aA'

Step 5: A ' se β derive karenge

A' → AB | Bc | Ac

Step 6: Check karo A' deterministic hai ya nahi

Observe:

A' → AB
A' → Ac

In dono me common prefix A hai.

So fir se left factoring karna padega.

Step 7: Second Left Factoring

Common prefix = A

So

A' → A A''

Remaining:

β1 = B
β2 = c

So

A'' → B | c

Step 8: Ek rule aur add karna padega

A' → Bc

Kyuki original grammar me Bc bhi tha.

Final Deterministic Grammar

A → aA'

A' → AA'' | Bc

A'' → B | c

Start symbol = A

Example 2

Given Grammar

S → IETS
S → IETSES
S → a

E → b

Step 1: Non-Deterministic Productions Identify karo

Observe:

S → IETS
S → IETSES

Common prefix:

IETS

Step 2: Prefix aur Betas identify karo

α = IETS

Remaining parts:

β1 = ε
β2 = ES

Note:

ε = empty string

Step 3: Left Factoring Apply karo

Common prefix bahar nikaalenge.

S → IETS S'

Step 4: S' se β derive karenge

S' → ε | ES

Step 5: Remaining rule add karo

Original grammar me tha:

S → a

So final grammar me bhi add karna padega.

Final Deterministic Grammar

S → IETS S' | a

S' → ε | ES

E → b

Important Question

Kya deterministic grammar hone se parser accept karega?

Answer:

❌ Nahi

Kyuki grammar ambiguous hai.

Important Rule

Almost saare parsers:

  • Top-down parsers

  • Bottom-up parsers

ambiguous grammars reject karte hain.

Proof: Determinism Ambiguity Remove Nahi Karta

Ek string generate karte hain:

IETIETSES

Non-Deterministic Grammar Derivation

Starting:

S → IETS

Phir:

S → IETSES

String ban jaati hai.

Second Derivation

S → IETSES

Phir andar wale S se:

S → IETS

Same string phir generate ho jaati hai.

Result

Same string ke 2 different derivations mil gaye.

Matlab grammar:

Ambiguous hai

Deterministic Grammar me bhi Same Problem

Left factoring ke baad:

S → IETS S'

S' → ε | ES

Phir bhi same string ke multiple derivations possible hain.

Isliye:

✔ Non-determinism remove hua
❌ Ambiguity remove nahi hui

Important Concept

📌 Non-Determinism ≠ Ambiguity

Dono alag problems hain.

Real Meaning of the String

String:

IETIETSES

Actually represent karta hai programming structure:

if e then
    if e then s
else s

Yaha else kis if ke saath attach hoga ye clear nahi hai.

Isko bolte hain:

Dangling Else Problem

Two Interpretations

Case 1 (Correct)

Else attach hota hai nearest if se.

if e then
    (if e then s else s)

Case 2 (Wrong)

Else attach hota hai outer if se.

if e then
    if e then s
else s

Isliye grammar ambiguous ho jata hai.

Conclusion

Is session me:

1️⃣ Left Factoring Examples dekhe

Non-deterministic grammar → deterministic grammar

2️⃣ Important Proof

Determinism ambiguity remove nahi karta

Final Key Points (Exam)

✔ Left factoring → non-determinism remove karta hai
✔ Ambiguity remove nahi karta
✔ Ambiguity ka reason multiple parse trees hota hai
✔ Non-determinism ka reason common prefix hota hai



Left Factoring – Solved Problems (Set-2) Notes

Session Goal

Is session me 3 solved examples dekhenge jisme Left Factoring use karke non-determinism remove karenge.

Rule yaad rakho:

✅ Common prefix = α (alpha)
✅ Remaining part = β (beta)

Grammar ko convert karte hain:

A → αβ1 | αβ2 | αβ3

into

A → αA′
A′ → β1 | β2 | β3

Aur jab tak common prefix ho tab tak left factoring repeat karna padta hai.

Example 1

Given Grammar

S → aSSBS
S → aSASB
S → aBB
S → B

Step 1: Common Prefix Find

First 3 productions me:

Common prefix = a

So

α = a

Remaining parts:

β1 = SSBS
β2 = SASB
β3 = BB

Step 2: Left Factoring

Common prefix bahar nikaalenge.

S → aS′

Step 3: S′ me β daalo

S′ → SSBS | SASB | BB

Also original grammar me tha:

S → B

So final rule:

S → aS′ | B

Step 4: Check S′ me non-determinism

Observe:

S′ → SSBS
S′ → SASB

Common prefix = S

Step 5: Second Left Factoring

S′ → S S″

Remaining parts:

β1 = SBS
β2 = ASB

So

S″ → SBS | ASB

Final Deterministic Grammar

S → aS′ | B

S′ → SS″ | BB

S″ → SBS | ASB

Important Concept

Left factoring tab tak karte hain jab tak koi common prefix na bache.

Example 2

Given Grammar

S → BSSAAS
S → BSSASB
S → BSB
S → a

Step 1: Common Prefix Identify

First 3 productions:

Common prefix = BS

So

α = BS

Remaining parts:

β1 = SAAS
β2 = SASB
β3 = B

Step 2: Left Factoring

S → BS S′

Step 3: S′ me β add

S′ → SAAS | SASB | B

Original rule:

S → a

So final:

S → BSS′ | a

Step 4: Check S′ me non-determinism

Observe:

SAAS
SASB

Common prefix = SA

Step 5: Second Left Factoring

S′ → SA S″

Remaining parts:

β1 = AS
β2 = SB

So

S″ → AS | SB

Also

S′ → B

Final Deterministic Grammar

S → BSS′ | a

S′ → SAS″ | B

S″ → AS | SB

Example 3

Given Grammar

S → a
S → ab
S → abc
S → abcd

Step 1: Common Prefix

Sab productions me:

Common prefix = a

Also:

a = aε

So betas:

β1 = ε
β2 = b
β3 = bc
β4 = bcd

Step 2: Left Factoring

S → aS′

S′ → ε | b | bc | bcd

Step 3: Check S′ me non-determinism

Observe:

b
bc
bcd

Common prefix = b

Step 4: Second Left Factoring

S′ → bS″

Remaining parts:

β1 = ε
β2 = c
β3 = cd

Also

S′ → ε

Step 5: Check S″

S″ → ε | c | cd

Common prefix:

c

Step 6: Third Left Factoring

S″ → cS‴

Remaining parts:

β1 = ε
β2 = d

Also

S″ → ε

Final Deterministic Grammar

S → aS′

S′ → bS″ | ε

S″ → cS‴ | ε

S‴ → d | ε

Important Rule (Exam)

📌 Left Factoring repeatedly apply karna padta hai

Jab tak:

❌ koi common prefix ho

Tab tak:

✅ factoring continue

Final Concept Summary

Left Factoring ka purpose:

➡ Non-determinism remove karna

Process:

1️⃣ Common prefix find karo
2️⃣ Prefix ko factor out karo
3️⃣ Remaining part new non-terminal me daalo
4️⃣ Check again for common prefixes
5️⃣ Repeat until grammar deterministic ho jaye


Parsers (Syntax Analyzer) – Notes

1. Parser kya hota hai?

Definition

Parser ek program hota hai jo:

➡ given string ke liye parse tree generate karta hai
➡ agar wo string underlying grammar se generate ho sakti hai

Simple words me

Parser check karta hai:

  • string grammar follow karti hai ya nahi

  • aur parse tree build karta hai

2. Compiler me Parser ka Role

Compiler ke phases:

1️⃣ Lexical Analyzer
2️⃣ Parser (Syntax Analyzer)
3️⃣ Semantic Analysis etc.

Example:

C code → Lexical analyzer

Convert ho jata hai tokens me:

id = id + id

Ye string parser ko di jati hai

Parser:

✔ grammar check karta hai
✔ parse tree banata hai

Agar string grammar se generate nahi ho sakti:

❌ syntax error

3. Parse Tree kaise Generate hota hai?

Parse tree generate karne ke 2 approaches hain:

1️⃣ Top-Down Approach
2️⃣ Bottom-Up Approach

4. Example Grammar

Assume grammar:

S → a A B e
A → A b c | a
B → d

Generate karna hai string:

a a b c d e

5. Top-Down Approach

Idea

Start from start symbol

S

Aur step-by-step string derive karte hain.

Step 1

Production:

S → a A B e

Derivation:

a A B e

Isse string ka:

  • first a

  • last e

cover ho gaya.

Step 2

Ab expand karenge A

Production options:

A → A b c
A → a

Hume string me b c chahiye.

Isliye choose karte hain:

A → A b c

Step 3

Remaining A

Use rule:

A → a

Now:

a a b c B e

Step 4

Expand B

B → d

Final string:

a a b c d e

Important Observation

Har step me expand hua:

➡ Leftmost non-terminal

Isliye:

Top-Down Approach = Leftmost Derivation

Top-Down Parser Decision

Parser ko decide karna padta hai:

✔ kaunsa production use kare

Galat rule choose hua toh string generate nahi hogi.

6. Bottom-Up Approach

Top-down ka opposite.

Idea

Start karte hain string se

Aur reduce karte hain start symbol tak.

Given String

a a b c d e

Step 1

Production:

A → a

So:

a → A

Step 2

Production:

A → A b c

So:

A b c → A

Step 3

Production:

B → d

So:

d → B

Step 4

Production:

S → a A B e

So:

a A B e → S

Finally:

S

Important Concept

Bottom-Up parsing actually:

Rightmost Derivation in Reverse

Kyuki:

✔ derivation rightmost hoti hai
✔ lekin reverse order me

Bottom-Up Parser Decision

Parser decide karta hai:

➡ kab reduce karna hai

7. Summary of Approaches

ApproachStartDerivation
Top-DownStart symbolLeftmost
Bottom-UpStringRightmost (reverse)

8. Parser Classification

Parsers 2 types ke hote hain:

1️⃣ Top-Down Parsers

Top-down approach follow karte hain.

Types

1. With Backtracking

Parser galat rule choose kare toh:

➡ backtrack karta hai

Example:

Brute Force Parser

2. Without Backtracking

Better and faster.

Types:

1️⃣ Recursive Descent Parser

2️⃣ Predictive Parser

Examples:

  • LL(1)

  • LL(k)


9. Bottom-Up Parsers

Inko bolte hain:

Shift-Reduce Parsers

Types

1️⃣ Operator Precedence Parser

2️⃣ LR Parsers

10. Types of LR Parsers

LR parsers 4 types ke hote hain:

1️⃣ LR(0)
2️⃣ SLR(1)
3️⃣ LALR(1)
4️⃣ CLR(1)

Power comparison:

LR(0) < SLR(1) < LALR(1) < CLR(1)

CLR(1) sabse powerful.

11. Important Restrictions

Ambiguous Grammar

Except:

✔ Operator Precedence Parser

Baaki parsers:

❌ ambiguous grammar handle nahi karte.

Non-Deterministic Grammar

Top-down parsers without backtracking

❌ handle nahi karte

Isliye:

✔ Left factoring karna padta hai

Left Recursion

Top-down parsers:

❌ left recursion handle nahi karte

Isliye:

✔ left recursion remove karna padta hai

12. Chapter Focus

Is chapter me:

Focus hoga:

✔ Top-Down Parsers without Backtracking

Next chapter me:

✔ Bottom-Up Parsers

Quick Revision (Exam)

Parser

Program jo parse tree generate karta hai

Parse Tree Generation

2 ways:

1️⃣ Top-Down
2️⃣ Bottom-Up

Top-Down

✔ Leftmost derivation
✔ Start symbol se start

Bottom-Up

✔ Rightmost derivation (reverse)
✔ String se start

Parser Types

Top-Down:

  • Recursive Descent

  • Predictive (LL)

Bottom-Up:

  • Operator Precedence

  • LR Parsers


Recursive Descent Parser – Notes (Top-Down Parser)

1. Previous Session Recap

Parsers ke types:

1️⃣ Top-Down Parsers

2️⃣ Bottom-Up Parsers

Top-down parsers ke bhi 2 types hote hain:

1️⃣ With Backtracking
2️⃣ Without Backtracking

Is course me focus:

➡ Top-Down Parser Without Backtracking

Jab bhi “Top-Down Parser” bole →
Without backtracking wala hi assume karna hai

2. Top-Down Parser Concept

Top-Down Parser:

✔ Top-Down approach use karta hai
✔ Parse tree start symbol se start hoti hai

Derivation type

Top-Down parser use karta hai:

➡ Leftmost Derivation

Matlab:

Har step me leftmost non-terminal expand hota hai

Parser ka main decision

Parser ko decide karna hota hai:

➡ Kaunsa production rule use kare

3. Conditions for Top-Down Parser

Top-Down parser ke liye grammar me:

❌ Left Recursion nahi hona chahiye

Example:

A → Aα

Isko remove karna padta hai.

❌ Non-Determinism nahi hona chahiye

Common prefix problem.

Solution:

✔ Left Factoring

4. Recursive Descent Parser


Definition

Recursive descent parser:

➡ Top-Down parser hai
➡ recursive procedures ka set use karta hai

Important point:

✔ Har non-terminal ke liye ek function / procedure hota hai

Meaning

Grammar:

Non-terminal → function

Example:

E → procedure E()
A → procedure A()
B → procedure B()

Isliye:

Program structure grammar jaisa hi hota hai

5. Example Grammar

E → i E'
E' → + i E' | ε

Symbols meaning:

SymbolMeaning
iidentifier
+plus operator
εepsilon (empty)


6. Recursive Descent Parser Idea

Har non-terminal → procedure

Toh procedures honge:

E()
E'()
match()
main()

7. Procedure for E

Production:

E → i E'

Procedure:

E()
{
 if(lookahead == i)
 {
   match(i)
   E'
 }
}

Meaning:

1️⃣ input me i check karo
2️⃣ match karo
3️⃣ E' call karo

8. Procedure for E'

Production:

E' → + i E'
E' → ε

Procedure:

E'()
{
 if(lookahead == +)
 {
   match(+)
   match(i)
   E'
 }
 else
   return
}

Meaning:

Agar + mila

→ + match
→ i match
→ E' again call

Agar + nahi mila

→ ε (empty)
→ return

9. Match Function

Match function:

➡ input symbol ko check karta hai

match(c)
{
 if(lookahead == c)
    lookahead = next input symbol
 else
    error
}

Meaning:

✔ agar symbol match ho gaya → next input read
❌ nahi hua → syntax error

10. Lookahead Variable

Lookahead:

➡ input stream ka current symbol

Example input:

i + i $

$ → input end symbol

Lookahead:

i → + → i → $

11. Main Function

Main function:

main()
{
 E()

 if(lookahead == $)
   print "Parsing Successful"
}

Meaning:

1️⃣ Start symbol call karo
2️⃣ Agar end $ aa gaya
3️⃣ Parsing successful

12. Input Example

Input:

i + i $

13. Parsing Steps

Step 1

Main → call E

Parse tree start:

E

Step 2

Production:

E → i E'

Match:

i

Lookahead next:

+

Tree:

E
├─ i
└─ E'

Step 3

E' check karega:

Lookahead:

+

Production:

E' → + i E'

Tree:

E
├─ i
└─ E'
    ├─ +
    ├─ i
    └─ E'

Step 4

Match:

+

Lookahead next:

i

Step 5

Match:

i

Lookahead next:

$

Step 6

Call E' again

Check:

lookahead == +

But now:

lookahead = $

Condition false.

So:

E' → ε

14. Final Parse Tree Yield

Yield:

i + i ε

Which equals:

i + i

Input accepted.

15. End Check

Main function check:

lookahead == $

True.

Output:

Parsing Successful

16. Final Concept

Recursive Descent Parser:

✔ Top-Down parser
✔ Backtracking nahi hota
✔ Non-terminals → procedures
✔ Leftmost derivation use karta hai

17. Important Conditions

Recursive descent parser ke liye grammar:

✔ Unambiguous
✔ No left recursion
✔ No non-determinism

18. Key Idea

Grammar:

Non-terminal → Procedure

Example:

E → E()
E' → E'()

Parser program grammar ko mirror karta hai.

Quick Exam Revision

Recursive Descent Parser

Top-down parser using:

➡ Recursive procedures

Key Properties

✔ Leftmost derivation
✔ No backtracking
✔ One function per non-terminal

Required Grammar

❌ Left recursion
❌ Non-determinism
❌ Ambiguity


LL(1) Parser – Short Notes (Compiler Design)

1. Types of Top-Down Parsers

Top-down parsers without backtracking are mainly of two types:

  1. Recursive Descent Parser

  2. Predictive Parser (LL(1) Parser)

The LL(1) parser is an improved predictive version of recursive descent parsing.

2. Why It Is Called Recursive Descent Parser

In recursive descent parsing:

  • Every non-terminal → function

  • Functions call each other recursively

  • The call stack (recursion stack) stores activation records.

Since:

  • Parsing is top-down

  • Parse tree grows from start symbol downward

Hence the name:

Recursive + Descent Parser

3. Organization of an LL(1) Parser

An LL(1) parser has three main components:

1️⃣ Input Buffer

  • Stores the input string

  • Ends with $ (end marker)

Example:

id + id $

2️⃣ Stack

Used by the parser during parsing.

Initial stack:

$ S

where S = start symbol

3️⃣ LL(1) Parsing Table

A table used to decide which production rule to apply.

It is built using:

  • FIRST set

  • FOLLOW set

4. Meaning of LL(1)

LL(1) stands for:

SymbolMeaning
First LScan input Left → Right
Second LLeftmost derivation
1Uses 1 lookahead symbol

Example lookahead:

id + id $
^

The parser checks only one symbol ahead to make decisions.

5. FIRST Function

Definition

Given any non-terminal of a CFG, if we derive all the possible strings

from it, the first terminal(s) is the FIRST() of the non-terminal.

FIRST(X) = set of terminals that appear first in strings derived from X.

Example 1

Grammar:

S → aABC
A → b
B → c
C → d

FIRST sets:

FIRST(S) = { a }
FIRST(A) = { b }
FIRST(B) = { c }
FIRST(C) = { d }

Because terminals appearing first are taken.

Example 2

Grammar:

S → ABC
A → a | ε
B → b
C → c

Find FIRST(S)

Step 1
Check A

A → a

So

FIRST(S) = { a }

Step 2
But A can also derive ε

So check next symbol B

B → b

So

FIRST(S) = { a , b }

Final:

FIRST(S) = { a , b }

6. FOLLOW Function

Definition

During the process of derivation, the terminal(s) that could follow the
non-terminal are to be considered as FOLLOW() of the non-terminal.

FOLLOW(A) = set of terminals that can appear immediately after A in some derivation.

Important rule:

$ ∈ FOLLOW(Start Symbol)

Example

Grammar:

S → ABC
A → a | ε
B → b | ε
C → c

FOLLOW(S)

Start symbol always contains:

FOLLOW(S) = { $ }

FOLLOW(A)

A is followed by B C

Step 1
Check FIRST(B)

FIRST(B) = { b , ε }

So add b

Step 2
Because B → ε
Check FIRST(C)

FIRST(C) = { c }

Final:

FOLLOW(A) = { b , c }

FOLLOW(B)

B is followed by C

FIRST(C) = { c }

So

FOLLOW(B) = { c }

FOLLOW(C)

C is the last symbol in production.

So add FOLLOW(S)

FOLLOW(C) = { $ }

7. Important Notes

FIRST Set

  • Contains terminals

  • May contain ε

Example:

FIRST(A) = { a , ε }

FOLLOW Set

  • Contains only terminals

  • Never contains ε

Example:

FOLLOW(A) = { b , c , $ }

8. Why FIRST and FOLLOW Are Needed

They are used to construct the LL(1) parsing table.

Parsing table helps the parser decide:

Which production rule to apply

without backtracking.

9. Key Exam Points

Remember these:

  • LL(1) parser = predictive parser

  • Uses FIRST and FOLLOW

  • Left to right scanning

  • Leftmost derivation

  • One lookahead symbol

  • Grammar must be:

    • No left recursion

    • Left factored

    • Unambiguous


FIRST and FOLLOW – Detailed Notes (LL(1) Parser)

1. Grammar Used in the Lecture

The grammar used for explanation is the standard expression grammar:

E  → T E'
E' → + T E' | ε
T  → F T'
T' → * F T' | ε
F  → id | ( E )

This grammar is:

  • Right recursive

  • Unambiguous

  • No left recursion

  • No nondeterminism

These conditions are required for LL(1) parsing.

2. Why FIRST is Calculated from Bottom Non-Terminals

In LL(1) grammars:

  • Production rules start with the start symbol

  • Lower rules often directly produce terminals

Example:

F → id | (E)

Here terminals appear immediately, so it is easier to compute FIRST(F) first.

Hence we start from the bottom of the grammar.

3. FIRST Set – Step by Step

Rule Reminder

FIRST(X) contains:

  • First terminal derived from X

  • ε if X can derive epsilon

Step 1: FIRST(F)

F → id | ( E )

First terminals are:

FIRST(F) = { id , ( }

Step 2: FIRST(T')

T' → * F T' | ε

From first rule:

* F T'

First terminal = *

Second rule:

ε

So:

FIRST(T') = { * , ε }

Step 3: FIRST(T)

T → F T'

F appears first.

So:

FIRST(T) = FIRST(F)

Therefore:

FIRST(T) = { id , ( }

Step 4: FIRST(E')

E' → + T E' | ε

First terminal:

+

Also epsilon.

FIRST(E') = { + , ε }

Step 5: FIRST(E)

E → T E'

First symbol = T

So:

FIRST(E) = FIRST(T)

Therefore:

FIRST(E) = { id , ( }

Final FIRST Sets

Non-TerminalFIRST
E{ id , ( }
E'{ + , ε }
T{ id , ( }
T'{ * , ε }
F{ id , ( }

4. FOLLOW Set – Step by Step

Important Rule

$ ∈ FOLLOW(Start Symbol)

FOLLOW set never contains ε.

Step 1: FOLLOW(E)

E is the start symbol.

So:

FOLLOW(E) = { $ }

Also from:

F → ( E )

E is followed by ).

Therefore:

FOLLOW(E) = { $ , ) }

Step 2: FOLLOW(E')

E' appears in:

E → T E'

E' is the last symbol, so:

FOLLOW(E') = FOLLOW(E)

Therefore:

FOLLOW(E') = { $ , ) }

Step 3: FOLLOW(T)

T appears in:

E → T E'
E' → + T E'

In both cases T is followed by E'.

So include:

FIRST(E') = { + , ε }

Add +.

If ε occurs, take FOLLOW of left side.

Left sides are:

E
E'

Both have:

{ $ , ) }

Therefore:

FOLLOW(T) = { + , $ , ) }

Step 4: FOLLOW(T')

T' appears in:

T → F T'

T' is the last symbol, so:

FOLLOW(T') = FOLLOW(T)

Therefore:

FOLLOW(T') = { + , $ , ) }

Step 5: FOLLOW(F)

F appears in:

T → F T'
T' → * F T'

In both cases F is followed by T'.

So include:

FIRST(T') = { * , ε }

Add *.

Since ε exists, include:

FOLLOW(T)
FOLLOW(T')

Which are:

{ + , $ , ) }

Final:

FOLLOW(F) = { * , + , $ , ) }

Final FOLLOW Sets

Non-TerminalFOLLOW
E{ $ , ) }
E'{ $ , ) }
T{ + , $ , ) }
T'{ + , $ , ) }
F{ * , + , $ , ) }

5. Important Rules for FOLLOW

Rule 1

If:

A → αBβ

Then:

FIRST(β) - {ε} ⊆ FOLLOW(B)

Rule 2

If:

A → αB

or

A → αBβ and FIRST(β) contains ε

Then:

FOLLOW(A) ⊆ FOLLOW(B)

6. Key Points for Exams

Remember these:

✔ FIRST may contain ε
✔ FOLLOW never contains ε
✔ Start symbol FOLLOW always contains $
✔ FIRST and FOLLOW are used to build LL(1) parsing table
